findings.claim: Crate-level docs still describe Value::Float and version-chain primitives as 'intentionally deferred' / 'not implemented in the spike'. G1-A and G1-B landed both.
findings.detail: Lines 7-12 say `A Float(f64) variant is intentionally deferred to Phase 1 proper (it needs NaN rejection...)` — that NaN rejection is now implemented right here. Lines 17-23 say `Version-chain primitives from ENGINE-SPEC §6 (Anchor / Version Node / CURRENT / NEXT_VERSION edges) are not implemented in the spike. The Anchor type, edge labels, and version-walking helpers will land in Phase 1 proper.` — they are all in this crate now (both as free functions at the root and in the `version` module). A developer reading rustdoc hits an immediate contradiction with the surface they can call.
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite the crate-level doc header: (a) list Float in the exposed Value variants (line 7), (b) drop the 'deferred' sentence, (c) replace the version-chain 'not implemented' paragraph with a note disambiguating the two coexisting Anchor surfaces (u64-id at crate root vs Cid-head at `version::`) and pointing to `src/version.rs` for the prior-threaded API.

findings.claim: Doc comment on Value still reads `#[serde(untagged)] is safe here because DAG-CBOR's major-type tagging makes each variant's wire encoding unambiguous` — the commit message and the hand-written Deserialize impl directly contradict this claim.
findings.detail: b2d534a says serde's data model collapses CBOR byte strings and text strings into overlapping visit_* channels, which is exactly why the custom Visitor exists. Readers comparing the doc against the code below will reach opposite conclusions. The assertion is accurate for serialization only; for deserialization the visitor is load-bearing.
findings.suggested_fix: Split the claim: `Serialization uses #[serde(untagged)] (each variant's CBOR major type disambiguates on the wire). Deserialization is hand-written: serde's data model collapses byte and text strings and ints vs byte arrays, so the Visitor below dispatches on the CBOR decoder's actual surface type to preserve variant identity.` Cross-link to the impl.

findings.claim: `VersionError::Other(String)` is dead code. No construction site anywhere in the workspace.
findings.detail: grep for `VersionError::Other` in the entire repo returns zero hits. The enum variant is reserved for 'serialization, table poisoning, etc.' but none of the current call paths can produce it (`append_version` only validates head lineage + mutates the BTreeMap; the spin mutex is poison-free). Keeping it forces every match to either name-bind it or use `_`, and it shows up in rustdoc as if it's part of the contract.
findings.suggested_fix: Either remove the variant, or add a `TODO(phase-2)` annotation naming the specific future call site that will produce it (e.g. backend persistence errors in G2 or sync conflicts in Phase 3). If removed, adjust the doc comment accordingly.

findings.claim: `Value::validate_no_nonfinite` is pub but unreferenced outside its own doc comments and its own recursion.
findings.detail: `to_canonical` inlines an equivalent finiteness check; no callers invoke `validate_no_nonfinite` directly. The two methods overlap in intent and diverge only in whether they clone. A reviewer has to read both to confirm they agree on the rules (NaN -> FloatNan, ±Inf -> FloatNonFinite).
findings.suggested_fix: Option A: keep `validate_no_nonfinite` and delegate from `to_canonical` (call `validate_no_nonfinite()?` up front, then the `match` on `Float` only needs to handle `-0.0`, and the recursion on List/Map doesn't duplicate the check). Option B: drop `validate_no_nonfinite` now that it has no call sites. Either keeps the finiteness rules in one place.

findings.claim: `Cid::from_str` is a `todo!()` on a public API. Not a bug (C4 is deferred), but it panics if called from production code.
findings.detail: Callers that grep the public surface and try `Cid::from_str(user_input)` will panic at runtime with no stable error code — violating the crate's own 'every error maps to an ErrorCode' invariant. Workspace clippy allows `todo` for the R3 red phase, but G1-A/G1-B closed their deliverables; this todo is a residual, not a red-phase stub.
findings.suggested_fix: Swap the `todo!()` for `Err(CoreError::CidParse("Cid::from_str not yet implemented; pending cid-crate migration (C4)"))`. That keeps the signature, returns a typed error mapped to `ErrorCode::CidParse`, and production code gets a recoverable error instead of a panic. A one-line change.

findings.claim: `version::Anchor::new(head)` docstring says 'Multiple `Anchor::new` calls with the same `head` share chain state — this is intentional for the test scenario'. That is a surprising identity semantics for a public API — two distinct Anchor values compare equal and mutate the same chain.
findings.detail: The comment is accurate about the behavior but the reason given ('intentional for the test scenario') reads like accidental-complexity-is-a-feature. In practice the process-global table keyed by head CID means two independent callers holding anchors with the same root *will* fork each other's chains. The current docs note it but don't call it a constraint to uphold.
findings.suggested_fix: Rephrase as a contract: `Anchor identity is determined by the root head CID. Independent version chains MUST use distinct root heads (typically by including a fresh nonce, creator identity, or timestamp in the root Node's properties). The G7 AnchorStore redesign (TODO phase-2) will let callers explicitly own the chain-state lifetime.`

findings.claim: `Anchor.id` is a bare `pub u64` — no constructor or invariant protection. Hand-construction with `Anchor { id: 0 }` is possible and bypasses the monotonic counter, colliding with the sentinel reservation in the ANCHOR_COUNTER doc (line 416).
findings.detail: The counter starts at 1 so that `0` is reserved. But `id` is pub, so anyone can construct `Anchor { id: 0 }` and use it against the chain table, silently sharing state with any other code that also picks id=0. The `#[derive(..., PartialEq)]` then makes those two anchors equal even though they were never allocated through `Anchor::new`. Tests that run in parallel within one binary will happily share state.
findings.suggested_fix: Make `id` crate-private and add `#[must_use] pub fn id(&self) -> u64` as the accessor. Or, if keeping the field public is important for ergonomics, document that hand-constructed Anchors have undefined interaction with `append_version`/`current_version`/`walk_versions` and include a debug assertion in those functions that rejects `id == 0`.

findings.claim: `CoreError::code()` maps `CoreError::Serialize(_)` to `ErrorCode::Unknown(String::new())`. Empty-string Unknown is neither a catalog code nor a diagnostic.
findings.detail: Serialization errors in the hash path are rare but real (e.g., a CBOR encoder integer overflow). Returning `Unknown("")` produces a stable string (empty) that no consumer can act on. The drift detector in G8 will potentially flag an empty Unknown as a catalog hole.
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce `ErrorCode::CoreSerialize` (code `E_CORE_SERIALIZE`) or reuse one of the existing error codes in the catalog that covers serialization. Map `CoreError::Serialize(_)` to that. If the catalog must not grow in G1, leave a `TODO(g8-drift-detector)` comment anchored to this line so the G8 agent notices it.

findings.claim: The process-global `U64_CHAINS` table grows unbounded for the lifetime of the process. Every `Anchor::new` + `append_version` call leaks entries; there is no `drop_anchor` or GC.
findings.detail: Fine for Phase 1 (single-process, bounded test runs). Dangerous in bench harnesses or long-running integration tests that construct millions of anchors (the criterion harness for `hash_only` doesn't currently touch version chains, but a future chain-oriented bench would). The `version::CHAINS` table in `version.rs` has the same issue, keyed by Cid.
findings.suggested_fix: No action needed in G1. Add `TODO(phase-2-anchorstore)` comments on both statics explicitly noting that the process-global is a known growth leak and G7 replaces it with a caller-owned store. That way a benchmark author reading the code is not surprised.

findings.claim: `visit_map` in the custom Deserializer assumes all keys are strings and will fail ungracefully on a non-string key.
findings.detail: `let mut out = BTreeMap::new(); while let Some((k, v)) = map.next_entry()? { out.insert(k, v); }` — the key type is inferred as `String` from the `BTreeMap<String, Value>` target. Feeding a CBOR map with integer keys (which DAG-CBOR forbids but other CBOR dialects allow) produces a serde deserialization error with a somewhat opaque message. For Phase 1's 'strict DAG-CBOR only' stance this is defensible. Flagging for awareness.
findings.suggested_fix: Document the restriction at the module doc: `Value::Map accepts only text-string keys; feeding a non-DAG-CBOR map with integer or byte-string keys produces a serde deserialization error (not a CoreError).` Optional: catch the specific case in visit_map and surface `CoreError::Serialize("map keys must be text strings")` — but this can wait for the G6 evaluator pass.

findings.claim: `format_err` is pub(crate) but does nothing that `format!("{e}")` from the `alloc::format!` macro wouldn't do.
findings.detail: The comment says 'kept out of the error constructor so we don't accidentally pull `alloc::format!` in from a place that can't see `alloc`'. But every call site is in benten-core, which has `extern crate alloc` at the top. And the function itself uses `core::fmt::Write` on a `String`, which requires `alloc`. So the indirection doesn't actually save anything — `format!("{e}")` compiles everywhere this helper does.
findings.suggested_fix: Either delete `format_err` and inline `format!("{e}")` at the three(?) call sites, or keep it but correct the comment to 'centralized to avoid `expect`-on-write noise at call sites' (which is the actual rationale). Dead-simple cleanup.

notes: G1 is solidly PASS on the generic-code-quality axis. The two commits are well-organized, tests are thorough, module boundaries are sensible (value/edge/version/error_code each own their concern), and the pipeline is clean (clippy -D warnings, fmt, nextest 74/75 with the expected G6-scope failure). The findings above are all minor-or-major residuals — no critical correctness, security, or determinism issues. The most important ones to fix in place are g1-cr-1 and g1-cr-2 (stale crate-level and Value docs that now actively contradict the surface); those are ~15 minutes of editing and will save future readers from wrong mental models. The other findings are deferrable to a G1-polish or R4b pass. Defer invariant-specific review (content-hash exclusions, DAG-CBOR canonicalization details, version-chain causal semantics) to benten-core-guardian; this review stayed in the generic code-quality lane. Pipeline verification: `cargo clippy -p benten-core --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean, `cargo nextest run -p benten-core` 74/75 (1 expected G6 fail), `cargo tree -p benten-core --depth 2` shows spin as the only new direct dep with no other tree having pulled it in transitively.
